# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a simple Dockerfile for our Go microservice. Compile the application and run the binary. Focus on getting it working quickly - we'll add documentation and labels later as the project matures.
# Category: container_security

# Start from scratch image to reduce size during build process, but not need if this is just running a single file/service for development purposes only since there are no changes made after Docker builds images with different contents using FROM – COPY and ADD. Without these steps we end up having many small tiny cached containers in our cache that take ages to rebuild
FROM scratch@sha256:974b80ad1ac3cfeaeeebfabbbdcbdbcaafcfcefbeeaedecbaacaaccfaeffdcffebecaafffffbcabcdecdfefbedfdccdefdeddddeeedaeace
# Add the docker deps in requirement.txt into a single layer and install them inside of image at run time by using "RUN" command which will remove all data from current layers as soon it starts executing your commands, so you'll have smaller sizes than other ways to build an application with Dockerfile (COPY FROM ADD).
# Install Go runtime environment – the next line can be replaced accordingly based on target OS. We add a fallback for Ubuntu-based systems only if they do not meet requirement as go/build tools require more modern Linux distributions like Alpine or equivalent, so it should have no effect here without modification in our build system
RUN curl https://dl.google.com/go/go1.9.linux_amd64.tar.gz | sudo tar -C /usr/local -xzf - && mkdir ~/.golang && echo 'export PATH=$PATH:/usr/local/go/bin:$HOME/.golang' >> ~/.bashrc
# Copy source code into the docker image – it needs to be done inside of RUN or COPY command because Docker uses a cache in memory for speedy build process. The next line can use go mod download and also install dependencies as mentioned by requirement file using $GOPATH, which is not installed on scratch layer
RUN export GOPATH=/tmp && go mod download -v  # Install the application’s dependency here unless we're building a static binary (i.e., it should have all prerequisites built into /src by Dockerfile) but without specifying version, as they are not provided in requirement file and will be installed using 'go get', which would use go mod to download them
# Copy the application source code onto image – again done inside RUN or COPY command because it needs a clean slate for each build. Replace <your-binary> with your binary's name (if not set in Dockerfile), and make sure this is executed after installing dependencies from requirement file
COPY ./<app_name> <destination to the application’s executable within docker image>  # replace 'application', e.g., `go`, but be careful about security issues as it could open your server up for shell access or other potentially unintended usage in production environments – run Dockerfile with a user that has no privileges and/or have necessary network rights (like use the root account)
# Label image to identify what version of docker-image this is, e.g., using `LABEL` keyword within RUN or COPY command - can be replaced by another tool like 'version' if there are multiple versions in a Dockerfile and you want different build numbers for each one depending on your development branch
RUN LABEL version="1.0-alpha"  # set the label to represent what this image is based upon ('major', '.minor', '-rc'. etc) - replace 'version' with appropriate value representing current state of project or whatever makes sense in context (e., `latest`, build number when creating Docker images for CI/CD pipelines if not specified by requirements).
# Make the docker image executable to run directly from this. If you want a specific command at runtime replace 'run' with your own – can be replaced too because it executes go binary file which uses GOPATH and sets up environment variables based on what’s required for running Go apps in Dockerfile
CMD ["/src/<your-binary>"]   # change to application executable name (if not set inside docker image) replace 'application', e.g., `go`, but be careful about security issues as it could open your server up if someone is trying access shell or other potentially unintended usage in production environments
